summary: O'Neil recounts the serendipitous moment when her Occupy group was drafting an op-ed against Larry Summers becoming Fed Chair, only to learn mid-meeting that he had withdrawn his name from consideration. She celebrates this as a small victory while acknowledging it's just one item on a longer list of financial reform goals. The post serves as both a celebration and a reminder that systemic issues like Too Big To Fail banks remain unresolved five years after the 2008 financial crisis.
key_points: O'Neil's Occupy group was actively organizing against Larry Summers' potential Fed Chair nomination when news broke of his withdrawal
The group was preparing for Occupy Wall Street's second anniversary, including releasing a book and holding a press conference at Zuccotti Park
Summers' withdrawal is framed as a victory but only one step on a longer path to financial reform
The post pivots to advocate for ending Too Big To Fail policies as the next priority
Five years after the 2008 crisis, O'Neil argues not enough structural change has occurred
money_quotes: And yes, it's a pretty awesome moment when exactly what you're working towards comes true like that, even if it's only one thing on a very long list.
Too Big To Fail needs to end, people.
It's been 5 years since the crisis, and not enough has changed.
Another in our group had blown away my milquetoast logical argument with a couple of paragraphs of pure Occupy outrage.
key_insight: Small activist victories matter and should be celebrated, but they must fuel continued organizing rather than complacency about deeper systemic problems.
